pattern: Swapping a window changes one array's sum by the windowed total of (other - self), so the best swap is the maximum-subarray gain over those differences, applied to whichever base sum it improves.
hints: Swapping a subarray adds the difference (nums2[i] - nums1[i]) over that window to sum(nums1), and the opposite to sum(nums2).
The most you can add to one array's sum is the largest contiguous-window total of those per-index differences, which is a maximum-subarray problem.
Run Kadane on (nums2[i] - nums1[i]) to boost nums1's sum, and on (nums1[i] - nums2[i]) for nums2's sum; the answer is the better of the two boosted sums.

edge_inputs.description: single beneficial swap in the middle
single element where swapping may help

stress.description: random arrays of equal length in the value range, sized so the quadratic oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    a = [rng.randrange(1, 10001) for _ in range(n)]
    b = [rng.randrange(1, 10001) for _ in range(n)]
    return [a, b]
stress.note: exercises the linear Kadane scan at scale
